title: Easter Calculator - Find Easter Sunday Date
tagline: Calculate Easter Sunday and related Christian holiday dates for any year using the precise Meeus/Jones/Butcher algorithm.
intro: Enter any year from 1583 onwards to instantly find Easter Sunday plus Ash Wednesday, Good Friday, Pentecost, and six other moveable feasts.

error.outOfRange: Year must be between 1583 and 9999 for the Gregorian calendar.

about.heading: About the Easter Calculator
about.body:
Easter Sunday is the most important and ancient feast in the Christian calendar, celebrating the resurrection of Jesus Christ. Unlike fixed holidays such as Christmas, Easter is a moveable feast — its date shifts every year, falling anywhere between 22 March and 25 April. This variability flows from a decision made at the First Council of Nicaea in 325 AD: Easter would be observed on the first Sunday following the Paschal Full Moon, which is defined as the first ecclesiastical full moon occurring on or after the spring equinox of 21 March.

Because that definition blends astronomical observation with ecclesiastical rule, calculating the exact date requires more than a simple look-up table. Over the centuries, mathematicians and astronomers devised several algorithms for computing the Paschal Full Moon without consulting actual astronomical tables. The most widely trusted modern formula is the Meeus/Jones/Butcher algorithm, which this calculator implements.

The algorithm, developed by Jean Meeus and refined by Ron Jones and Thomas Butcher, works entirely in integer arithmetic. It begins by finding the year's position in the 19-year Metonic cycle (the golden number), which governs how lunar phases repeat relative to the solar calendar. It then applies a series of correction factors — including a century correction that adjusts for the drift of the Gregorian calendar — to pinpoint the Paschal Full Moon. Finally it advances the date to the following Sunday to obtain Easter Sunday.

All the moveable feasts shown by this calculator are derived from Easter Sunday by fixed offsets. Ash Wednesday, the start of Lent, falls 46 days before Easter. Palm Sunday marks the beginning of Holy Week, seven days before Easter. Good Friday commemorates the crucifixion two days before Easter. Easter Monday, the day after Easter, is a public holiday in many countries. Ascension Day, celebrating Christ's ascent into heaven, falls 39 days after Easter (always a Thursday). Pentecost, marking the descent of the Holy Spirit, is 49 days after Easter. Trinity Sunday follows one week later, 56 days after Easter.

The algorithm is valid for all years in the Gregorian calendar, which was introduced in October 1582 and first applied to Easter calculations from 1583. For years before 1583 a different calculation based on the Julian calendar would be needed, which is why this calculator validates the year input against that lower bound.

Beyond religious observance, accurate Easter dates are essential for academic calendar planning in countries where schools follow the church year, for liturgical print production that must be completed months in advance, for retail and travel businesses that plan promotions around the Easter holiday period, and for software systems that need to mark public holidays automatically. This calculator gives planners and developers a fast, reliable reference that requires no subscription or external API.

howto.heading: How to Use the Easter Calculator
steps: Type a year between 1583 and 9999 into the Year field — any year in the Gregorian calendar is supported.
Click Calculate Easter Date. The tool runs the Meeus/Jones/Butcher algorithm and instantly displays Easter Sunday plus seven related moveable feasts.
Read off Ash Wednesday if you need the start of Lent, Palm Sunday for Holy Week, or Good Friday for public holiday planning.
Use the Ascension Day, Pentecost, and Trinity Sunday dates for liturgical or academic calendars that extend past Easter.
Click Reset to clear the result and calculate for a different year.

items.q: Why does Easter fall on a different date each year?
items.a: Easter is defined as the first Sunday after the Paschal Full Moon — the first ecclesiastical full moon on or after 21 March. Because the lunar cycle does not align perfectly with the solar year, the Paschal Full Moon shifts relative to the calendar, moving Easter anywhere between 22 March and 25 April.

It is a compact integer-arithmetic formula developed by Jean Meeus and refined by Jones and Butcher that calculates Easter Sunday for any year in the Gregorian calendar. It accounts for the 19-year Metonic cycle, century corrections, and ecclesiastical rules, producing the same result as official church tables without needing any astronomical lookup.

items.q: Why is 1583 the earliest supported year?
items.a: The Gregorian calendar was introduced in October 1582 and first used for Easter calculations starting in 1583. Before that date, the Julian calendar was used, which requires a different algorithm. Entering a year before 1583 in this calculator would produce incorrect results, so it validates the input.

items.q: How are Ash Wednesday and the other dates calculated?
items.a: All moveable feasts are derived from Easter Sunday by adding or subtracting a fixed number of days. Ash Wednesday is 46 days before Easter, Palm Sunday is 7 days before, Good Friday is 2 days before, Easter Monday is 1 day after, Ascension Day is 39 days after, Pentecost is 49 days after, and Trinity Sunday is 56 days after Easter.

items.q: Is Eastern Orthodox Easter the same date?
items.a: No. The Eastern Orthodox Church follows the Julian calendar for Easter calculation, which produces dates that often differ from the Western Gregorian date by one to five weeks. This calculator uses only the Gregorian method. In some years the two traditions coincide, but the Julian algorithm is a separate calculation.

items.q: Can I use this calculator for future planning years in advance?
items.a: Yes. The algorithm is fully deterministic and works for any year in the Gregorian calendar up to 9999. Liturgical publishers, academic institutions, and travel businesses routinely plan three to five years ahead using this method. Simply enter the target year and copy the dates into your planning documents.
